answer:
Yes, "wisdom" serves as a solid single-word gloss for all four terms across contexts, capturing their shared sense of profound insight, sagacity, or higher knowing (whether experiential, prudent, intuitive, or salvific). It aligns well with standard scholarly renderings: *prajñā* and *jñāna* as transcendent or discriminative wisdom/knowledge in Indic traditions; *frōd* (often "sage" or "wise elder," implying age-earned depth); *wītig* (often "wise," "prudent," or "knowing").[^1][^2]

### Representative Passages
**Old English *frōd* ("frod")**: In *Elene*, it describes David as a "frod fyrnweota" (wise ancient counselor), emphasizing venerable, lore-rich insight passed to Solomon.[^3]  
> Be þām Dauid cyning dryhtleoth agol, frod fyrnweota, fæder Salomones...

The modern translation renders this as David the king... wise ancient counselor.[^4]

**Old English *wītig* ("witig")**: In *Andreas*, priests are "witig werede" (wise assembly/host), speaking with authority amid wonders.[^5]  
> Sæpte sacerdas sweotolum tacnum, witig werede ond worde cwaeth...

**Sanskrit *prajñā*/*jñāna***: The Falsafa corpus lacks direct English translations invoking these terms (no hits in Smṛti texts like Aṅgirasa or Bṛhaspati, which use "wisdom" more generically for ethical/discriminative insight). A loose analogue appears in *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, where "wisdom" burns sin like fire.[^6]  
> Burned by the triple fire of their wisdom, it is destroyed...

### Where "Wisdom" Creaks
It strains most with *frōd*'s connotation of age-earned maturity (not mere intellect), as in *Andreas*: "nalas wintrum frod" (not wise/seasoned in winters/years). Here, "wisdom" flattens the youth-vs.-experience binary into abstract sagacity, losing the temporal depth—contrast *prajñā*'s atemporal gnosis or *wītig*'s quick prudence.[^7]  
> Þū eart seolfa geong... nalas wintrum frod...

[^1]: *Cynewulf, Elene*, Ch. 1 (transliteration), [p-624562](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/1/transliteration/#p-624562).  
[^2]: *Cynewulf, Andreas*, Ch. 1 (transliteration), [p-992aeb](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/1/transliteration/#p-992aeb).  
[^3]: *Cynewulf, Elene*, Ch. 1 (transliteration), [p-624562](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/1/transliteration/#p-624562).  
[^4]: *Cynewulf, Elene*, Ch. 1 (translation), [p-624562](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/1/translation/#p-624562). (Prose summary derived from full chapter context.)  
[^5]: *Cynewulf, Andreas*, Ch. 1 (transliteration), [p-992aeb](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/1/transliteration/#p-992aeb).  
[^6]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1 (translation), [p-ac401b](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/1/translation/#p-ac401b).  
[^7]: *Cynewulf, Andreas*, Ch. 1 (transliteration), [p-992aeb](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/1/transliteration/#p-992aeb).
